response:
RULING: You can play a provisional ball under Rule 18.3 if you believe your original ball may be:

1) Lost outside a water hazard, or
2) Out of bounds.

To play a provisional ball, you must announce that you are playing it before taking any other actions. You must also play the provisional ball before you go forward to search for the original ball.

The player has three options for proceeding:

1) If the original ball is found in bounds, you must continue play with that ball.
2) If the original ball is lost or out of bounds, you must continue with the provisional ball.
3) If after a reasonable search the original ball is not found, the provisional ball becomes the ball in play under penalty of stroke and distance.

The provisions for playing a provisional ball are detailed in Rule 18.3 of the Rules of Golf.

response:
RULING: You may take free relief from an embedded ball in the general area of the course, but not in a bunker or penalty area.

The Rules of Golf provide for free relief from an embedded ball through the green, meaning in the general area. Rule 16.3a states that if your ball is embedded in its own pitch-mark in any closely-mown area through the green, you may take relief without penalty by dropping the ball within one club-length of the spot where the ball was embedded, not nearer the hole.

However, this relief is not available if your ball is embedded in a bunker or penalty area. In those cases, you would either need to play the ball as it lies or take penalty relief per the specific rules for those hazards.

This ruling applies in both match play and stroke play formats.

The player is entitled to free relief from the casual water under Rule 16.1.

response:
RULING: The player whose ball is in a difficult or unplayable position is the sole judge of whether to declare their ball unplayable.

According to Rule 19.1 of the Rules of Golf, "The player may declare their ball unplayable at any place on the course, except when the ball is in a penalty area." The player is the only one who can make this determination - the Rules do not give this authority to any other person, including a rules official, playing partner, or opponent.

If a player deems their ball to be unplayable, they must take relief under Rule 19.2 or Rule 19.3. This involves either taking stroke-and-distance relief, back-on-the-line relief, or lateral relief, with a one-stroke penalty. The player can select whichever relief option works best for their situation.

response:
RULING: If your opponent deliberately moves your ball marker in match play, the penalty is loss of hole.

The Rules of Golf state that in match play, if a player lifts or moves an opponent's ball marker without the opponent's authority, the penalty is loss of hole (Rule 15.3a).

The opponent has committed a serious breach of the rules by deliberately interfering with your ball position. The proper penalty is the loss of the current hole being played, which is the most severe match play penalty available.

You should inform your opponent of the ruling and the penalty. The marker must then be replaced in its original position before play continues.

response:
RULING: No, you cannot replace the cracked driver shaft during your round.

Under Rule 4.1b(3) of the Rules of Golf, a player is generally not allowed to replace a club that is damaged during a round unless the damage was not the player's fault. In this case, since the driver shaft cracked not from abuse, the player would be permitted to replace the damaged club.

However, the replacement club must be one that the player (or someone in their group) is carrying as part of their 14-club limit for the round. The player cannot go get a different driver from their car to use as a replacement. Doing so would be a breach of Rule 4.1b(4) which prohibits adding a club during the round.

The player's options in this situation are:
1) Continue the round using the damaged driver, even with the cracked shaft.
2) If the player has a backup driver in their golf bag, they could replace the damaged driver with that one.
3) If the player does not have a suitable replacement, they would have to play the remainder of the round with 13 clubs.

Rule 19-1 covers the options for an unplayable ball. Since your ball is in an impossible lie in the woods, you have the following options under this rule:

1. Play the ball as it lies
2. Drop the ball within two club-lengths of where the ball lies, but not nearer the hole 
3. Drop the ball on a straight line from the hole through the spot where the ball lay, with no limit to how far back you go

Since you have decided your ball is unplayable, you can choose to go back to the tee and proceed under stroke-and-distance per option 3 above. The fact that your caddie threw down a ball for lateral relief does not require you to play that ball, as long as you have not made a stroke at it.

The key is that you have not played the caddie-dropped ball yet. Once you make a stroke at a ball, you are committed to that relief option. But since that has not happened here, you can still choose to go back to the tee under the unplayable ball rule.
